EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate flooding of the Vermillion River, and of several other small streams. Flooding of farmland was reported, including some farmland away from the Vermillion River. Several roads were flooded and some of the roads were damaged. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused light to moderate flooding of the Vermillion River in the northern part of the county. Some small streams in the county were also flooded, as well as farmland and lowland across the county. A few roads were closed by the flooding, and water was running over other roads. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010. The river flooding ended before the end of the month, but some lowland flooding continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail at a few locations in southeast South Dakota during the early evening of April 9th. There was also one report of damaging winds.

Major flooding of the Big Sioux River, other streams, lakes, and general flooding, which began with a rapid March snowmelt, continued through April. Water levels, having fallen from March peaks, rose again at various times during the month, and were generally falling again by the end of the month. However, any fall of water levels was very slow. Widespread flooding of farmland continued. Many roads remained flooded with heavy road damage being reported in some areas. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that improvement was so slow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of lakes and lowlands in the county, continued through April, though flooding generally abated. Some farmland remained flooded, both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. The Big Sioux River crested at 2.2 feet above flood stage at the North Cliff Avenue station in Sioux Falls on April 16th, a crest lower than the major flooding of March. A few roads remained covered by water, and there was some road damage. High water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010, a main reason the flooding onset was so fast in March, was also a main reason that the flooding subsided so slowly during April.

High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that the flooding either grew worse or improved so slowly.

Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the Big Sioux River, and of many small streams and lakes. Widespread flooding of farmland was also reported, including farmland both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. Numerous roads were flooded and some of the roads suffered heavy damage. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate to major flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county. Farmland was flooded, especially near the Big Sioux River, and other lowlands were flooded. The Big Sioux River crested at 8.3 feet above flood stage across from Hawarden, Iowa on March 26th. Several roads were closed by the flooding, and water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the Big Sioux River. The flooded roads included numerous rural roads. Some roads were heavily damaged, but the amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. Some homes and businesses were also flooded. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the Big Sioux River, other streams, lakes, and general flooding, which began with a rapid March snowmelt, continued through April. Water levels, having fallen from March peaks, rose again at various times during the month, and were generally falling again by the end of the month. However, any fall of water levels was very slow. Widespread flooding of farmland continued. Many roads remained flooded with heavy road damage being reported in some areas. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that improvement was so slow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of lakes and lowlands in the county, continued through April, though small stream flooding abated and flooding in other areas such as the city of Flandreau abated some. Some farmland remained flooded, both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. A few roads remained closed by the flooding for part of the month. Water was covering some other roads, mainly from flooded lowlands. Some roads were damaged. High water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010, a main reason the flooding onset was so fast in March, was also a main reason that the flooding subsided so slowly during April.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 10 inches included light accumulations which fell before and after the heavy snow during the daytime hours of January 31st. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions that developed on New years Eve continued through New Years morning, abating by noon. Winds gusting to over 40 mph and fresh snow cover of 6 to 10 inches produced the blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 6 inches, most of which fell the previous day, and winds gusting to over 40 mph produced blowing snow. Visibilities were lowered frequently to below a mile, and locally to a quarter mile or less. The storm continued from the previous day which was New Years Eve. Travel was made very difficult and some businesses and roads were closed, including Interstate 29. Conditions were improving by noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow produced heavy accumulations of 8 to 10 inches in an area near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during a 24 hour period beginning in the late afternoon of January 9th. Lesser accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were reported further north and west in southeast South Dakota. An exposure fatality was reported in Sioux Falls during the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 7 inches was accompanied by light to moderate winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds caused blowing and drifting snow over an area which extended from Brookings County South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Cold temperatures and wind chills approaching 20 degrees below zero developed during the event and continued through the night as the winds and blowing snow slowly decreased. There was a fatality from exposure in Brookings County during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting above 35 mph caused blowing and drifting of existing snow cover. Visibilities dropped below a half mile at times, and drifting snow blocked some roads. Wind chills lowered to near 20 below zero during the night. A 65 year old woman died of exposure after she left her vehicle which had become stuck in drifts on a township road near Elkton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high wind gust of 66 mph occurred at Pierre with no damage reported.
A mid February warm up caused ice break-up and subsequent ice jam flooding along the White River from south of Interior to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River.  The river gage on the Highway 83 bridge north of White River, SD crested at 14.7 feet or 0.7 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid February warm up caused ice break-up and subsequent ice jam flooding along the White River from south of Interior to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White River fluctuated above and below flood stage for several days causing minor flooding to occur.  The river gage southwest of Oacoma along Highway 47 crested at 21.4 feet or 6.4 feet above flood stage. Flooding of agricultural land occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Falling temperatures and strong north to northwest winds produced dangerous wind chills throughout the first day of February and on the morning of February 2nd. The extreme cold came as a system which produced snow and blowing snow at the end of January moved east away from the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds averaging 15 to 30 mph combined with temperatures dropping below zero to produce wind chills of 20 to 35 below zero on February 1st. On the night of February 1st and the early morning of February 2nd, wind chills reached the 35 to 40 below zero range at times despite slowly decreasing winds, as temperatures fell further below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the Rockies and Northern Plains, bringing heavy snow to western and south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts were in the six to 12 inch range, with as much as 18 inches from northwestern into west central South Dakota. Northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph produced areas of blowing snow and greatly reduced visibilities at times. Interstate 90 east of Rapid City was closed during the overnight hours.

Northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph produced areas of blowing snow and greatly reduced visibilities at times. Interstate 90 east of Rapid City was closed during the overnight hours.

An intense upper level low pressure area moved across the region Sunday into Monday morning bringing very heavy snowfall to all of the region. Snowfall amounts of 10 to over 20 inches along with north winds of 25 to 40 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions and heavy drifting across the region. The heavy snow and low visibilities resulted in road closures Sunday into Monday. Interstate-29 and Interstate-90 were both closed on Sunday and not opened until Monday. Interstate-29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border while Interstate-90 was closed from Wall to Chamberlain.  Businesses, flights, schools, and many events were closed or cancelled Sunday and Monday.  At several locations, this snowstorm set all-time record snowiest calender days in February along with top five all-time snowiest calender days for the season. Finally, the February snowfall totals were in the top five all-time for most locations. ||Snowfall amounts across the region included 10 inches at Pollock and Selby; 11 inches at Mobridge, Kennebec, and Onida; 12 inches at Pierre, Blunt, Castlewood, and Eagle Butte.  Locations with more than a foot of snowfall included 13 inches at Hayti, Roscoe, and Eureka; 14 inches at Ipswich, Clear Lake, Clark, Mission Ridge, Mclaughlin, Sisseton, Gann Valley, Gettysburg and Victor; 15 inches at Bryant, Bowdle, Watertown, and Leola; 16 inches at Faulkton, Wilmot, and Summit; with 17 inches at Andover.  Locations with 18 inches or more included, 18 inches at Aberdeen, Redfield, and Stratford; 20 inches at Turton and Ree Heights; 21 inches at Milbank; 22 inches at Miller and 23 inches near Highmore. The snowfall began in the early morning hours of the 20th and ended around noon on the 21st.

Blizzard conditions developed along and east of the Sisseton Hills late on February 2nd and continued into the mid morning hours of February 3rd. Strong southwest winds of 30 to 40 mph gusting to around 55 mph picked up the existing snow cover causing blizzard conditions which wreaked havoc along Interstate-29.  Whiteout conditions and heavy drifting brought traffic to a halt along a stretch of Interstate-29 from north of Wilmot to Sisseton. One-hundred fifty to two-hundred vehicles were stranded along this stretch.  A full scale rescue operation ensued during the night and continued into the next day.  Interstate-29 was closed from Watertown to the North Dakota border as it took most of the day to clean up all of the stalled vehicles. There were also many accidents along the stretch of the interstate with many people stranded for up to twelve hours. There were also many other people stranded on secondary roads who had to be rescued. No injuries occurred as a result of this incident. The Roberts County Emergency Manager was stranded and conducted  emergency operations from his vehicle. Interstate-29 was reopened by the evening of February 3rd.

Widespread moderate to heavy precipitation fell as heavy snow over the northern and western part of the area, and as freezing rain and sleet in far southeast South Dakota. Up to 12 1/2 inches of snow and up to a half inch of glaze from freezing rain were reported in places, with over an inch of sleet accumulation in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 11 inches, including at least 10 inches at Madison, severely limited travel and commerce, with some roads becoming blocked. Winds averaging 20 to 30 mph contributed to the problems by causing drifting snow and areas of reduced visibilities in blowing snow. The snow was mixed with sleet and freezing rain at the start of the storm.

A widespread and deep snow pack began to melt in early March across central and northeast South Dakota. The runoff brought many rivers and creeks across the area over flood stage. Many roads were flooded along with many acres of crop and pastureland.  Road and bridge damage occurred along with some outbuildings being damaged.  A Presidential Disaster was declared for much of the area. Much of the flooding continued into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding on the Big Sioux River occurred from near Watertown to the southern part of the county. The river went above flood stage on March 20th and continued above flood stage into April at the gage below Watertown. The river rose from 1 to 1 1/2 feet above flood stage. Agricultural land along with few roads were flooded.

A deep and expansive snow pack across the area began to melt bringing many areas of flooding to central and northeast South Dakota from mid through late March. The flooding continued into April. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland were flooded. Roads, culverts, and bridges were damaged across the region. Several roads were washed out with many closed. Many homes were threatened with some surrounded by water. Many people had to use four-wheelers to get to their homes. A Presidential Disaster was declared for all of the counties do to the flooding damage.  The damage estimates were from 4.5 to 5 million dollars for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding occurred across parts of Spink county flooding many roads along with agricultural and cropland. The Snake Creek from Redfield and south went above the flood stage of 11.5 feet on March 22nd rising to 17.5 feet on the March 24th. The creek continued to flood into April. Also, the rising Redfield Lake threatened four homes where sandbagging took place.

An upper level disturbance brought snow to southwestern South Dakota during the night. Most areas received two to five inches of snow, but a heavier band of snow developed across Shannon, Jackson, and Bennett Counties and produced as much as nine inches of snow in an area from Porcupine to Kadoka to Martin.

Several ice jams caused minor flooding along the White River from northwest of White River to the Missouri River during the last half of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River. The river gage on the U.S. Highway 83 bridge north of the town on White River crested at 14.7 feet, 0.7 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several ice jams caused minor flooding along the White River from northwest of White River to the Missouri River during the last half of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White River fluctuated above and below flood stage for several days, causing minor flooding of agricultural land. The river gage southwest of Oacoma along SD Highway 47 crested at 21.4 feet, or 6.4 feet above flood stage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and sleet produced a quarter to a half inch of glaze, topped by similar sleet accumulations. Travel and walking surfaces were made very icy, and the ice accumulations caused an unknown amount of damage to trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The melting of the deep snow pack began across the region which brought rising levels on all lakes across Hamlin county, especially Lake Poinsett. The lakes were already high going into the winter. Sandbagging efforts began for properties near the rising lakes. The lakes began to flood several roads and highways across the county. With the continued snow melt and rising lakes into April, more road and property damage ensued, especially on Lake Poinsett. See April storm data.

A deep and expansive snow pack across the area began to melt bringing many areas of flooding to central and northeast South Dakota from mid through late March. The flooding continued into April. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland were flooded. Roads, culverts, and bridges were damaged across the region. Several roads were washed out with many closed. Many homes were threatened with some surrounded by water. Many people had to use four-wheelers to get to their homes. A Presidential Disaster was declared for all of the counties do to the flooding damage.  The damage estimates were from 4.5 to 5 million dollars for the area.

Widespread moderate to heavy precipitation fell as heavy snow over the northern and western part of the area, and as freezing rain and sleet in far southeast South Dakota. Up to 12 1/2 inches of snow and up to a half inch of glaze from freezing rain were reported in places, with over an inch of sleet accumulation in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 8 inches, including 7 inches at Platte,  severely limited travel and commerce over the northwest part of the county, with some roads becoming blocked. Winds averaging 20 to 30 mph contributed to the problems by causing drifting snow and areas of reduced visibilities in blowing snow. The snow was preceded by freezing rain and sleet, causing icing of travel surfaces. In the southeast part of the county, the precipitation was mainly freezing rain and sleet, with considerable icing of driving and walking surfaces, and some ice accumulation on trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to heavy precipitation fell as heavy snow over the northern and western part of the area, and as freezing rain and sleet in far southeast South Dakota. Up to 12 1/2 inches of snow and up to a half inch of glaze from freezing rain were reported in places, with over an inch of sleet accumulation in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced around a half inch of glaze, topped by sleet accumulations of similar amounts in much of the county. Travel and walking surfaces were made very icy, and the ice accumulations caused an unknown amount of damage to trees and power lines. Damage was considerable in the Yankton area, where downed power lines produced an additional hazard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbances brought snow to much of western South Dakota. The heaviest snow developed over the northern Black Hills, where upslope northwest winds enhanced snowfall. Snowfall amounts of one to two feet were common, with the highest amounts from Lead and Deadwood southwestward to Cheyenne Crossing and O'Neill Pass. The gusty winds produced blowing snow, which greatly reduced visibility at times.

A strong area of low pressure moving across North Dakota brought high winds to parts of central and north central South Dakota. High winds of 30 to 50 mph with gusts to 70 to 80 mph occurred across the area. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 63 mph at Onida and 79 mph 10 miles south of Bullhead in Corson county.

An upper level low pressure area moving across the region brought 6 to 14 inches of snowfall across northeast South Dakota. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Groton, Clark, Clear Lake, and Stratford; 7 inches at Victor and Roy Lake; 8 inches at Sisseton and Webster; 9 inches at Britton; 10 inches at Waubay and Big Stone City; 11 inches at Summit and Milbank; and 14 inches at Wilmot.  Travel became difficult across the affected area with many schools and events cancelled.

An intense upper level low pressure area moved across the region Sunday into Monday morning bringing very heavy snowfall to all of the region. Snowfall amounts of 10 to over 20 inches along with north winds of 25 to 40 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions and heavy drifting across the region. The heavy snow and low visibilities resulted in road closures Sunday into Monday. Interstate-29 and Interstate-90 were both closed on Sunday and not opened until Monday. Interstate-29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border while Interstate-90 was closed from Wall to Chamberlain.  Businesses, flights, schools, and many events were closed or cancelled Sunday and Monday.  At several locations, this snowstorm set all-time record snowiest calender days in February along with top five all-time snowiest calender days for the season. Finally, the February snowfall totals were in the top five all-time for most locations. ||Snowfall amounts across the region included 10 inches at Pollock and Selby; 11 inches at Mobridge, Kennebec, and Onida; 12 inches at Pierre, Blunt, Castlewood, and Eagle Butte.  Locations with more than a foot of snowfall included 13 inches at Hayti, Roscoe, and Eureka; 14 inches at Ipswich, Clear Lake, Clark, Mission Ridge, Mclaughlin, Sisseton, Gann Valley, Gettysburg and Victor; 15 inches at Bryant, Bowdle, Watertown, and Leola; 16 inches at Faulkton, Wilmot, and Summit; with 17 inches at Andover.  Locations with 18 inches or more included, 18 inches at Aberdeen, Redfield, and Stratford; 20 inches at Turton and Ree Heights; 21 inches at Milbank; 22 inches at Miller and 23 inches near Highmore. The snowfall began in the early morning hours of the 20th and ended around noon on the 21st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt brought flooding across much of Marshall county. Many roads along with crop and pastureland were flooded. The lake levels also rose across the county. In the early morning hours of March 18th, the creek northeast of Langford became blocked by snow and ice, causing water to run into Langford streets and the house basements. Some people had a couple of feet of water in their basements. The basements of at least two homes in Langford had collapsed.  The water receded during the late afternoon.

A strong cold front passed through the area during the early morning. Northwest winds increased behind the front, with the strongest winds across far northwestern South Dakota. Sustained winds of 35 to 50 mph and gusts around 60 mph were recorded.

A storm system moved across the Central Rockies and Central Plains, bringing snow to western South Dakota. Most of western South Dakota received two to five inches of snow. A heavier band of snow developed across Harding and Butte Counties during the morning and produced five to ten inches of snow from Belle Fourche to Buffalo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very intense low pressure area moving across North Dakota brought widespread blizzard conditions to central and northeast South Dakota. The low pressure area brought 1 to 3 inches of snowfall to the region. This new snowfall combined with 30 to 50 mph winds with gusts to 60 to 70 mph brought widespread whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill with many motorists having to be rescued and taken to shelter. Hundreds of cars were stranded on mainly Highway 12 and Interstate-29. Two people traveling on Highway 10 in McPherson county told about how they became stuck and were picked up by another vehicle and that it took them over 2 1/2 hours to travel just a few miles to safety. Interstate-29 was closed from Watetown to Sisseton from 6 pm on the 11th until noon on the 12th.  Many events were affected including the Girls State Basketball Tournament in Watertown. There were several overturned semi trucks along with several vehicle accidents across the area. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 56 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge, Sisseton, and Faulkton; 59 mph at Aberdeen; 61 mph at Bowdle; 66 mph near Hillhead, and 71 mph west of Long Lake.

A 97-year old Aberdeen woman died from exposure to the cold when she went outside her apartment patio and the door locked behind her. The woman was believed to be outside for 8 to 10 hours. The overnight low was 2 degrees above zero. The winds averaged around 10 mph through the night giving a wind chill of around 10 below zero. Also, nearly an inch of snow occurred through the night.

Melting snow and resulting runoff caused minor flooding along the Big Sioux River, affecting small areas of pastureland and farmland in Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few days of melting snow in mild weather caused minor flooding along the Big Sioux River, affecting lowlands including farmland and pastureland on the South Dakota side of the river. The river crested at 1.3 feet above flood stage across from Hawarden, Iowa on February 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few days of mild weather caused snow to melt, resulting in minor flooding of Willow Creek near Sioux Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few days of melting snow in mild weather caused minor flooding of Willow Creek near Crooks, northwest of Sioux Falls. Mainly pastureland was affected. The creek crested at 1.9 feet above flood stage on February 18th..

Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the Big Sioux River, and of many small streams and lakes. Widespread flooding of farmland was also reported, including farmland both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. Numerous roads were flooded and some of the roads suffered heavy damage. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate to major flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county. Farmland was flooded, both near to and away from the Big Sioux River, and other lowlands were flooded. Numerous roads were closed by the flooding, and water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the Big Sioux River. The flooded roads included numerous rural roads. Some roads were heavily damaged, but the amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. Some homes and businesses were also flooded. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the Big Sioux River, and of many small streams and lakes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate to major flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county. Farmland was flooded, both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. Other lowlands were flooded, including parkland in Sioux Falls. The Big Sioux River crested at 5.55 feet above flood stage at North Cliff Avenue in Sioux Falls on March 25th. Numerous roads were closed by the flooding, and water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the Big Sioux River. The flooded roads included numerous rural roads, including many of the east and west roads between Dell Rapids and Interstate 90 near the Big Sioux River. Some roads were heavily damaged, but the amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. Some homes and businesses were also flooded. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure area moved across the region Sunday into Monday morning bringing very heavy snowfall to all of the region. Snowfall amounts of 10 to over 20 inches along with north winds of 25 to 40 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions and heavy drifting across the region. The heavy snow and low visibilities resulted in road closures Sunday into Monday. Interstate-29 and Interstate-90 were both closed on Sunday and not opened until Monday. Interstate-29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border while Interstate-90 was closed from Wall to Chamberlain.  Businesses, flights, schools, and many events were closed or cancelled Sunday and Monday.  At several locations, this snowstorm set all-time record snowiest calender days in February along with top five all-time snowiest calender days for the season. Finally, the February snowfall totals were in the top five all-time for most locations. ||Snowfall amounts across the region included 10 inches at Pollock and Selby; 11 inches at Mobridge, Kennebec, and Onida; 12 inches at Pierre, Blunt, Castlewood, and Eagle Butte.  Locations with more than a foot of snowfall included 13 inches at Hayti, Roscoe, and Eureka; 14 inches at Ipswich, Clear Lake, Clark, Mission Ridge, Mclaughlin, Sisseton, Gann Valley, Gettysburg and Victor; 15 inches at Bryant, Bowdle, Watertown, and Leola; 16 inches at Faulkton, Wilmot, and Summit; with 17 inches at Andover.  Locations with 18 inches or more included, 18 inches at Aberdeen, Redfield, and Stratford; 20 inches at Turton and Ree Heights; 21 inches at Milbank; 22 inches at Miller and 23 inches near Highmore. The snowfall began in the early morning hours of the 20th and ended around noon on the 21st.

A strong storm system moved across the Rockies and Northern Plains, bringing heavy snow to western and south central South Dakota. Snowfall amounts were in the six to 12 inch range, with as much as 18 inches from northwestern into west central South Dakota. Northwest winds of 15 to 30 mph produced areas of blowing snow and greatly reduced visibilities at times. Interstate 90 east of Rapid City was closed during the overnight hours.

A winter storm moved across the region, bringing snow to portions of western South Dakota. Five to ten inches of snow accumulated across southwestern South Dakota. A band of heavy snow developed across the southern Black Hills, where a few locations received around a foot of snow.

An upper level disturbance brought snow to southwestern South Dakota during the night. Most areas received two to five inches of snow, but a heavier band of snow developed across Shannon, Jackson, and Bennett Counties and produced as much as nine inches of snow in an area from Porcupine to Kadoka to Martin.

Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate flooding of the Vermillion River, and of several other small streams. Flooding of farmland was reported, including some farmland away from the Vermillion River. Several roads were flooded and some of the roads were damaged. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate flooding of the Little Vermillion River, as well as flooding of the East and West Fork Vermillion Rivers, and some small streams and lakes in the county. Farmland as well as other lowlands was flooded in some areas. The Little Vermillion River crested at 2.21 feet above flood stage near Salem on March 17th. A few roads were closed by the flooding, and water was running over other roads. A few roads were heavily damaged, but the amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010. The river flooding ended before the end of the month, but some lowland flooding continued.

Widespread moderate to heavy precipitation fell as heavy snow over the northern and western part of the area, and as freezing rain and sleet in far southeast South Dakota. Up to 12 1/2 inches of snow and up to a half inch of glaze from freezing rain were reported in places, with over an inch of sleet accumulation in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 9 to 12 inches, including 12 inches at Huron, severely limited travel and commerce, with some roads becoming blocked. Winds averaging 20 to 30 mph contributed to the problems by causing drifting snow and areas of reduced visibilities in blowing snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain produced around a half inch of glaze, topped by sleet accumulations of similar amounts in the northern two thirds of the county, with lesser ice to the south. Travel and walking surfaces were made very icy, and the ice accumulations caused an unknown amount of damage to trees and power lines.

A deep and expansive snow pack across the area began to melt bringing many areas of flooding to central and northeast South Dakota from mid through late March. The flooding continued into April. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland were flooded. Roads, culverts, and bridges were damaged across the region. Several roads were washed out with many closed. Many homes were threatened with some surrounded by water. Many people had to use four-wheelers to get to their homes. A Presidential Disaster was declared for all of the counties do to the flooding damage.  The damage estimates were from 4.5 to 5 million dollars for the area.

Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused flooding of small streams and lakes in several counties in southeast South Dakota. The flooding included farmland and other lowlands, with some roads flooded and damaged. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused flooding of lowlands and small streams, including considerable flooding of farmland. Several roads in the county were flooded. Some of the roads were closed, and some were washed out in spots. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread and deep snow pack began to melt in early March across central and northeast South Dakota. The runoff brought many rivers and creeks across the area over flood stage. Many roads were flooded along with many acres of crop and pastureland.  Road and bridge damage occurred along with some outbuildings being damaged.  A Presidential Disaster was declared for much of the area. Much of the flooding continued into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River went above flood stage in Spink county on March 20th continuing through the end of the month and into April. The river rose to 22 to 26 feet at both Ashton and Redfield or 6 to 9 feet above flood stage. These moderate to major stages flooded countless acres of crop and pastureland along with many roads.

A very intense low pressure area moving across North Dakota brought widespread blizzard conditions to central and northeast South Dakota. The low pressure area brought 1 to 3 inches of snowfall to the region. This new snowfall combined with 30 to 50 mph winds with gusts to 60 to 70 mph brought widespread whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill with many motorists having to be rescued and taken to shelter. Hundreds of cars were stranded on mainly Highway 12 and Interstate-29. Two people traveling on Highway 10 in McPherson county told about how they became stuck and were picked up by another vehicle and that it took them over 2 1/2 hours to travel just a few miles to safety. Interstate-29 was closed from Watetown to Sisseton from 6 pm on the 11th until noon on the 12th.  Many events were affected including the Girls State Basketball Tournament in Watertown. There were several overturned semi trucks along with several vehicle accidents across the area. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 56 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge, Sisseton, and Faulkton; 59 mph at Aberdeen; 61 mph at Bowdle; 66 mph near Hillhead, and 71 mph west of Long Lake.

A very intense low pressure area moving across North Dakota brought widespread blizzard conditions to central and northeast South Dakota. The low pressure area brought 1 to 3 inches of snowfall to the region. This new snowfall combined with 30 to 50 mph winds with gusts to 60 to 70 mph brought widespread whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill with many motorists having to be rescued and taken to shelter. Hundreds of cars were stranded on mainly Highway 12 and Interstate-29. Two people traveling on Highway 10 in McPherson county told about how they became stuck and were picked up by another vehicle and that it took them over 2 1/2 hours to travel just a few miles to safety. Interstate-29 was closed from Watetown to Sisseton from 6 pm on the 11th until noon on the 12th.  Many events were affected including the Girls State Basketball Tournament in Watertown. There were several overturned semi trucks along with several vehicle accidents across the area. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 56 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge, Sisseton, and Faulkton; 59 mph at Aberdeen; 61 mph at Bowdle; 66 mph near Hillhead, and 71 mph west of Long Lake.

A strong cold front passed through the area during the early morning. Northwest winds increased behind the front, with the strongest winds across far northwestern South Dakota. Sustained winds of 35 to 50 mph and gusts around 60 mph were recorded.

Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused flooding of small streams and lakes in several counties in southeast South Dakota. The flooding included farmland and other lowlands, with some roads flooded and damaged. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused flooding of lowlands, lakes, and small streams, including considerable flooding of farmland. Especially notable was the flooding of Lake Thompson. Numerous roads in the county were flooded. Some of the roads were closed, and some were washed out in spots. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread and deep snow pack began to melt in early March across central and northeast South Dakota. The runoff brought many rivers and creeks across the area over flood stage. Many roads were flooded along with many acres of crop and pastureland.  Road and bridge damage occurred along with some outbuildings being damaged.  A Presidential Disaster was declared for much of the area. Much of the flooding continued into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The James River went above flood stage throughout Brown county from Columbia to Stratford on March 22nd continuing through the end of the month and into April. The river rose above the flood stage of 13 to 14 feet to 3 to 4 feet above flood stage by the end of the month.  Agricultural and pastureland along with many roads were flooded. The river continued to rise into April to moderate to major stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure area moving across the region brought 6 to 14 inches of snowfall across northeast South Dakota. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Groton, Clark, Clear Lake, and Stratford; 7 inches at Victor and Roy Lake; 8 inches at Sisseton and Webster; 9 inches at Britton; 10 inches at Waubay and Big Stone City; 11 inches at Summit and Milbank; and 14 inches at Wilmot.  Travel became difficult across the affected area with many schools and events cancelled.

Widespread moderate to heavy precipitation fell as heavy snow over the northern and western part of the area, and as freezing rain and sleet in far southeast South Dakota. Up to 12 1/2 inches of snow and up to a half inch of glaze from freezing rain were reported in places, with over an inch of sleet accumulation in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 10 inches severely limited travel and commerce, with some roads becoming blocked. Winds averaging 20 to 30 mph contributed to the problems by causing drifting snow and areas of reduced visibilities in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure area moved across the region Sunday into Monday morning bringing very heavy snowfall to all of the region. Snowfall amounts of 10 to over 20 inches along with north winds of 25 to 40 mph brought widespread blizzard conditions and heavy drifting across the region. The heavy snow and low visibilities resulted in road closures Sunday into Monday. Interstate-29 and Interstate-90 were both closed on Sunday and not opened until Monday. Interstate-29 was closed from Sioux Falls to the North Dakota border while Interstate-90 was closed from Wall to Chamberlain.  Businesses, flights, schools, and many events were closed or cancelled Sunday and Monday.  At several locations, this snowstorm set all-time record snowiest calender days in February along with top five all-time snowiest calender days for the season. Finally, the February snowfall totals were in the top five all-time for most locations. ||Snowfall amounts across the region included 10 inches at Pollock and Selby; 11 inches at Mobridge, Kennebec, and Onida; 12 inches at Pierre, Blunt, Castlewood, and Eagle Butte.  Locations with more than a foot of snowfall included 13 inches at Hayti, Roscoe, and Eureka; 14 inches at Ipswich, Clear Lake, Clark, Mission Ridge, Mclaughlin, Sisseton, Gann Valley, Gettysburg and Victor; 15 inches at Bryant, Bowdle, Watertown, and Leola; 16 inches at Faulkton, Wilmot, and Summit; with 17 inches at Andover.  Locations with 18 inches or more included, 18 inches at Aberdeen, Redfield, and Stratford; 20 inches at Turton and Ree Heights; 21 inches at Milbank; 22 inches at Miller and 23 inches near Highmore. The snowfall began in the early morning hours of the 20th and ended around noon on the 21st.

A deep and expansive snow pack across the area began to melt bringing many areas of flooding to central and northeast South Dakota from mid through late March. The flooding continued into April. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland were flooded. Roads, culverts, and bridges were damaged across the region. Several roads were washed out with many closed. Many homes were threatened with some surrounded by water. Many people had to use four-wheelers to get to their homes. A Presidential Disaster was declared for all of the counties do to the flooding damage.  The damage estimates were from 4.5 to 5 million dollars for the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding again affected much of Brown county this year, especially in northeast Brown county in the Claremont region. Many roads again went under water along with countless acres of farmland. Also, several creeks rose to near or above flood stage including the Moccasin and Foote creeks. The Maple River in northwest Brown county also went above flood stage for a couple days at the end of the month. The Maple River went above the flood stage of 10 feet to 11.7 feet and then back below flood stage from the 24th to the 26th.

Major flooding of the James River, other streams, lakes, and general flooding, which began with a rapid March snowmelt, continued through April. Water levels with the more general areal flooding were at the highest at the start of April. However, the fall of water levels was very slow, with damage from flooding reported through the month.  Widespread flooding of farmland continued, also alleviating very slowly. Numerous roads remained flooded with heavy road damage being reported. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that improvement was so slow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the James River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county, continued through April. Much farmland remained flooded, both near to and away from the James River. The James River was 9.2 feet above flood stage near Yankton April 1st, and fell very slowly during the month. A large area of land and numerous roads were flooded at the start of the month. Water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the James River. Many roads were heavily damaged. Some homes and businesses were also flooded, with the flooding of these places slowly alleviating through the month. High water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010, a main reason the flooding onset was so fast in March, was also a main reason that the flooding subsided so slowly during April.

A large area of land and numerous roads were flooded at the start of the month. Water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the James River. Many roads were heavily damaged. Some homes and businesses were also flooded, with the flooding of these places slowly alleviating through the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding in March continued across much of central and northeast South Dakota as the rest of the snowpack melted into early April. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland remained flooded. Roads, culverts, and bridges were damaged across the region. Several roads were washed out with many closed. Many homes were threatened with some surrounded by water. Rising lake levels in northeast South Dakota also threatened and flooded many homes. Many people had to use four-wheelers to get to their homes. A Presidential Disaster was declared for all of the counties do to the flooding damage.  The total damage estimates, including March, were from 4.5 to 5 million dollars for the area.  The flooding diminished across much of the area into May.

Major flooding of the Big Sioux River, other streams, lakes, and general flooding, which began with a rapid March snowmelt, continued through April. Water levels, having fallen from March peaks, rose again at various times during the month, and were generally falling again by the end of the month. However, any fall of water levels was very slow. Widespread flooding of farmland continued. Many roads remained flooded with heavy road damage being reported in some areas. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that improvement was so slow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of lakes and lowlands in the county, continued through April, though small stream flooding abated. Much farmland remained flooded, both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. Flooding around the south end of Lake Pointsett in the northwest part of the county continued in particular to be damaging. The Big Sioux River crested at 3 feet above flood stage near Brookings on April 6th. Some roads remained closed by the flooding for part of the month. Water was running over other roads, from flooded lowlands, lakes, and the Big Sioux River. Some roads were heavily damaged. Some homes and businesses were still flooded. High water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010, a main reason the flooding onset was so fast in March, was also a main reason that the flooding subsided so slowly during April.

A very strong surface low pressure area moving across North Dakota brought high winds to much of central into parts of northeast South Dakota. Northwest winds of 35 to 50 mph with gusts to over 60 mph occurred from the morning to the late evening of the 30th. The high winds did cause some property damage across the region.  A semi was tipped over on Highway 50 in Buffalo county, a large sign was brought down in Highmore, with some damage to security lights and twisted traffic signals in Pierre. Some of the highest wind gusts included, 59 mph at Eagle Butte, 61 mph at Oacoma, 66 mph in Corson county, and 69 mph at Hayes in Stanley county.

Snowmelt flooding in March continued across much of central and northeast South Dakota as the rest of the snowpack melted into early April. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland remained flooded. Roads, culverts, and bridges were damaged across the region. Several roads were washed out with many closed. Many homes were threatened with some surrounded by water. Rising lake levels in northeast South Dakota also threatened and flooded many homes. Many people had to use four-wheelers to get to their homes. A Presidential Disaster was declared for all of the counties do to the flooding damage.  The total damage estimates, including March, were from 4.5 to 5 million dollars for the area.  The flooding diminished across much of the area into May.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt flooding damaged many roads and highways, including U.S. Highway 81, throughout Hamlin County.  Many roads were closed throughout the county. In the late evening of April 13th on U.S Highway 81, a car with four people inside went through a flooded area at a high rate of speed and ended up in the flooded ditch. They all got out with no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the deep snowpack across the region brought very high levels to area lakes across Hamlin County, especially Lake Poinsett.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The continued melting of the snowpack across the region brought rising levels on all lakes in Hamlin county. The high water level on Lake Poinsett resulted in the most significant property damage. Sandbagging efforts were ongoing for properties near the lake. The break up of the ice along with the strong winds brought large waves and ice chunks crashing into many homes.  Up to as many as fifty homes were affected by the high water, waves, and ice.  Some homes were completely leveled. Lake Poinsett, including several other lakes, flooded and damaged several county and township roads including U.S. Highway 81.  This flooding severely hampered travel in Hamlin county.  The flooding continued into May.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 5 to 8 inches in the northwestern part of the county, with lesser accumulations to the southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff caused all lakes to rise in Day county. The lakes most affected were Bitter, Rush, Blue Dog, and Waubay lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rising levels on Blue Dog, Bitter, Rush, and Waubay Lakes caused extensive road and property damage.  The town of Waubay was encroached upon by three lakes. Many families had to leave as their homes and cabins were surrounded by water or flooded. Ice breakup and high waves from the lakes also caused damage to homes and cabins. Also, hundreds of acres of farmland were swallowed up by the lakes. Many roads in Day county were flooded and closed with many of them damaged. Blue Dog Lake also inundated dozens of homes and roads and threatened water and sewer systems. Blue Dog, Bitter, and Waubay lakes all had reached record levels. Bitter Lake had grown to around 19 thousand acres which is up from around 3700 acres in the mid 1990s.

Mud creek near Rauville also went slightly above the flood stage of 9 feet to 9.64 feet for a couple days in early April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient over the region resulted in strong northwesterly winds across western and central South Dakota. Sustained winds of 35 to 55 mph with gusts near 80 mph caused minor damage around Newell and Sturgis. The strongest winds were over the northwestern South Dakota plains.
Wet snow accumulated 6 to 9 inches in scattered areas of southeast South Dakota, away from the eastern border of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated 2 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large surface low pressure area brought 1 to 2 inches of snowfall resulting in slushy roads and difficult driving conditions across Hamlin County. The poor conditions contributed to a fatal accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One male and one female in their late twenties were killed around 830 am CDT on the 15th on a slushy U.S. Highway 81.  The third person in the car was injured-non life threatening. The car they were driving was hit by a large truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the James River, other streams, lakes, and general flooding, which began with a rapid March snowmelt, continued through April. Water levels with the more general areal flooding were at the highest at the start of April. However, the fall of water levels was very slow, with damage from flooding reported through the month.  Widespread flooding of farmland continued, also alleviating very slowly. Numerous roads remained flooded with heavy road damage being reported. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that improvement was so slow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the James River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county, continued through April. Much farmland remained flooded, both near to and away from the James River. The James River was 5.8 feet above flood stage near Forestburg on April 1st, and fell very slowly during the month. A large area of land and numerous roads were flooded. Water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the James River. Many roads were heavily damaged. Some homes and businesses were also flooded, with the flooding of these places slowly alleviating through the month. High water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010, a main reason the flooding onset was so fast in March, was also a main reason that the flooding subsided so slowly during April.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding of the James River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county, continued through April. Much farmland remained flooded, both near to and away from the James River. The James River was 7.3 feet above flood stage at Huron on April 1st, and fell very slowly during the month. Over 50 miles of roads remained closed by the flooding at the start of the month, including South Dakota Highway 28 in the eastern part of the county. Water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the James River. Many roads were heavily damaged. The damage to township and county roads alone was estimated near the end of April at 1.7 million dollars. Some homes and businesses were also flooded, with the flooding of these places slowly alleviating through the month. High water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010, a main reason the flooding onset was so fast in March, was also a main reason that the flooding subsided so slowly during April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm moved across the Rockies and Central Plains, bringing rain and snow to western and south central South Dakota. The heaviest precipitation fell from southwestern into central South Dakota, where rain changed to snow during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. Snow continued during the night and into the morning of the 15th. Snowfall accumulations of six to 12 inches were common with locally higher amounts. Gusty northwest winds produced areas of blowing snow and reduced visibilities.

Falling temperatures and strong north to northwest winds produced dangerous wind chills throughout the first day of February and on the morning of February 2nd. The extreme cold came as a system which produced snow and blowing snow at the end of January moved east away from the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds averaging 15 to 30 mph combined with temperatures dropping below zero to produce wind chills of 20 to 35 below zero on February 1st. On the night of February 1st and the early morning of February 2nd, wind chills continued in this very cold range despite slowly decreasing winds, as temperatures fell further below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blizzard that started on New Year's Eve wound down during the morning hours of New Year's Day. Bitter cold northwest winds of 25 to 40 mph combined with additional snowfall of 6 to 10 inches brought visibilities to near zero across much of the region. This was the second blizzard in two days across the region. Both Interstates 29 and 90 were closed from December 31st until January 2nd. There were several stranded motorists along Highway 83 with five people being rescued.||The total snowfall amounts from the two storms at the end of December into New Year's Day ranged from 6 to 15 inches across the region.  The snowfall amounts included 6 inches at Eagle Butte; 7 inches at Doland; 8 inches at Mobridge and Gann Valley; 9 inches at Castlewood; 10 inches at Murdo, Clark, Ipswich, Kennebec, and Watertown and 11 inches at Clear Lake and Bryant.  Locations with a foot or more of snow included 12 inches at Aberdeen, Gettysburg, Highmore, Milbank, Mission Ridge, and Bowdle; 13 inches at Eureka, Pierre, Onida, and Blunt; 14 inches at Mellette, Sisseton, Victor, and Roscoe with 15 inches at Britton, Webster, and Redfield.  The snowfall began between 6 am and noon CST on the 31st and ended between 4 am and 11 am CST on the January 1st.

Snow produced heavy accumulations of 8 to 10 inches in an area near the Missouri River in southeast South Dakota during a 24 hour period beginning in the late afternoon of January 9th. Lesser accumulations of 4 to 8 inches were reported further north and west in southeast South Dakota. An exposure fatality was reported in Sioux Falls during the snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 5 inches was accompanied by light to moderate winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions that developed on New years Eve continued through New Years morning, abating by noon. Winds gusting to over 40 mph and fresh snow cover of 6 to 10 inches produced the blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 9 inches, most of which fell the previous day, and north to northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions with near zero visibilities which continued from the previous day which was New Years Eve. Many roads and businesses were closed by the storm with travel difficult to impossible. A decrease in falling snow and wind allowed conditions to improve by noon.

Blizzard conditions that developed on New years Eve continued through New Years morning, abating by noon. Winds gusting to over 40 mph and fresh snow cover of 6 to 10 inches produced the blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 9 inches, most of which fell the previous day, and north to northwest winds gusting to over 40 mph produced widespread blizzard conditions with near zero visibilities which continued from the previous day which was New Years Eve. Many roads and businesses were closed by the storm with travel difficult to impossible. A decrease in falling snow and wind allowed conditions to improve by noon.

The Missouri River, which had been near flood stage in many areas, began a significant rise near the end of the month in response to widespread upstream heavy rain which fell after unusually large upstream snowmelt. Flooding reached the moderate category by the end of May with the river on the way up across its length.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Missouri River developed and then increased in late May as runoff from excessive upstream snowmelt and rain reached the area. Lowlands flooded included camping and other recreational facilities including marinas next to Springfield. Roads flooded included some outlying Springfield streets. The river reached its highest May stage at about 3 feet above flood stage near Springfield and near Niobrara, Nebraska at the end of the month.

With record snowmelt runoff in the upper Missouri River Basin along with much above normal May precipitation, dam releases on the Missouri River were increased substantially by the end of May. The increased releases from Garrison Dam in North Dakota and Oahe Dam north of Pierre resulted in the Missouri River rising to above the flood stage of 13 feet at Pierre on May 24th. The Missouri River rose to 15.33 feet by the end of May. Full-scale flood mitigation operations began for Pierre and Fort Pierre with sandbagging and levee building.  Several thousand volunteers and National Guard soldiers helped out. The state EOC (Emergency Operations Center) was initiated in Pierre to handle all coordination of the flood operations. Several public areas were closed in Pierre including two parks. Several roads were also raised in Fort Pierre to keep access to as many homes as possible. Several homes began to flood in Fort Pierre by the end of the month. Releases from the Garrison and Oahe dams continued to increase into June and would reach historic/record levels. See June Storm Data.
Previous snowmelt and above normal May rainfall kept Lake Poinsett along with several other lakes well above full throughout May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water levels and the flooding of many homes continued on Lake Poinsett along with several other lakes across Hamlin county throughout May. The high water level on Lake Poinsett resulted in the most significant property damage. Lake Poinsett, including several other lakes, continued to flood and damage several county and township roads including U.S. Highway 81.  The flooding severely hampered travel in Hamlin county.  The flooding continued into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High lake levels from previous snowmelt and above normal May rainfall kept all lakes in Day county at record levels throughout the month. Extensive flooding of land and many residences continued throughout May. The flooding continued into June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Previous snowmelt and above normal May rainfall kept water levels high on Blue Dog, Bitter, Rush, and Waubay Lakes throughout the month. The high lake levels continued to cause extensive road and property damage.  Many families remained away from their homes and cabins. Also, hundreds of acres of farmland remained flooded along with many roads. Blue Dog, Bitter, and Waubay lakes were all at record levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds at several locations in southeast South Dakota during the late afternoon and early evening of May 21st.  The area affected extended from the Tyndall area just north of the Missouri River northeast to the Sioux Falls area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew off a metal porch awning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor to moderate flooding of the upper Big Sioux River continued in May but generally abated slowly. No new flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor to briefly moderate flooding of the upper Big Sioux River, ongoing since March snowmelt, continued in May but generally abated slowly. Some lowlands along the river were flooded, but no new flooding was reported. The highest stages on the river were 2.1 feet above flood stage near Bruce, and 1.7 feet above flood stage near Brookings, both at the start of the month.

Two surviving trees stopped one destroyed grain bin from slamming into the house on one farmstead, while the house on another farmstead suffered minor damage from debris, including a piece of lumber from one machine shed blown into the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Previous snowmelt and above normal May rainfall resulted in continued flooding on several rivers and creeks across northeast South Dakota throughout May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River above Watertown was above flood stage for a short time in early May before it went below flood stage on May 5th. The river then went above flood stage again on May 30th with the flooding continuing into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Previous snowmelt and above normal May rainfall resulted in continued flooding on several rivers and creeks across northeast South Dakota throughout May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal May rainfall kept the James River well above flood stage throughout Brown county from Columbia to Stratford through the month. Crop and pastureland along with several roads continued to be flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Previous snowmelt and above normal May rainfall resulted in continued flooding on several rivers and creeks across northeast South Dakota throughout May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal May rainfall kept the James River well above flood stage throughout Spink county through the month. Several roads along with crop and pastureland remained flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Previous snowmelt and above normal May rainfall resulted in continued flooding on several rivers and creeks across northeast South Dakota throughout May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Turtle Creek at Redfield went below flood stage on May 6th and remained below flood stage through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the James River, and of many small streams and lakes. Widespread flooding of farmland was also reported, including farmland both near to and away from the James River. Numerous roads were flooded and some of the roads suffered heavy damage. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the James River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county. Farmland was also flooded, both near to and away from the James River. The James River crested at 6.48 feet above flood stage northeast of Scotland on March 27th. Numerous roads were closed by the flooding including several major state and county roads. Water was running over other roads from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the James River. Some roads were heavily damaged, including in the Parkston area well west of the James River. The amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. Some homes and businesses were also flooded. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure area brought widespread heavy snowfall to central and parts of northeast South Dakota. This early spring storm brought 6 to 14 inches of heavy snow to the area.  The heavy wet snow caused a lot of travel problems along with a few accidents.  Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Gann Valley, Kennebec, Ipswich, Turton, Mobridge, Roscoe, and McLaughlin; 7 inches at Pollock, Groton, McIntosh, Miller, Mission Ridge, and Mellette; 8 inches at Blunt, Onaka, Selby, Murdo, and Timber Lake; 9 inches at Claremont, Gettysburg, Bowdle, 9 NW Britton; 10 inches at Faulkton; 11 inches at Onida, Ree Heights, and Artas.  Locations with a foot or more of snowfall included, 12 inches 12 SSW Harrold, 23 N Highmore, and Orient; 13 inches 14 NNE Isabel and Eureka with 14 inches at Eagle Butte.

A very intense low pressure area moving across North Dakota brought widespread blizzard conditions to central and northeast South Dakota. The low pressure area brought 1 to 3 inches of snowfall to the region. This new snowfall combined with 30 to 50 mph winds with gusts to 60 to 70 mph brought widespread whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill with many motorists having to be rescued and taken to shelter. Hundreds of cars were stranded on mainly Highway 12 and Interstate-29. Two people traveling on Highway 10 in McPherson county told about how they became stuck and were picked up by another vehicle and that it took them over 2 1/2 hours to travel just a few miles to safety. Interstate-29 was closed from Watetown to Sisseton from 6 pm on the 11th until noon on the 12th.  Many events were affected including the Girls State Basketball Tournament in Watertown. There were several overturned semi trucks along with several vehicle accidents across the area. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 56 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge, Sisseton, and Faulkton; 59 mph at Aberdeen; 61 mph at Bowdle; 66 mph near Hillhead, and 71 mph west of Long Lake.

A widespread and deep snow pack began to melt in early March across central and northeast South Dakota. The runoff brought many rivers and creeks across the area over flood stage. Many roads were flooded along with many acres of crop and pastureland.  Road and bridge damage occurred along with some outbuildings being damaged.  A Presidential Disaster was declared for much of the area. Much of the flooding continued into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff caused the Moreau River to rise above the flood stage of 21 feet on March 17th.  The river rose to near 27 feet on March 20th before falling quickly back  to below flood stage on March 21st.  Pasture and agricultural land along with a few roads were flooded as a result. Some homes were also threatened by the river. The ice on the river also broke several power poles leaving some people without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice break-up and several ice jams caused flooding along the White River near the Highway 47 bridge south of Reliance downstream to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the White River.  The river gage southwest of Oacoma along Highway 47 crested at 16.9 feet of 1.9 feet above flood stage.  Flooding of agricultural land occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice break-up and several ice jams caused flooding along the White River near the Highway 47 bridge south of Reliance downstream to the Missouri River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the White River. The river gage southwest of Oacoma along Highway 47 crested at 23.0 feet or 8.0 feet above flood stage. At least one home was threatened by the flooding.

Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused flooding of small streams and lakes in several counties in southeast South Dakota. The flooding included farmland and other lowlands, with some roads flooded and damaged. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused flooding of lowlands and small streams, including considerable flooding of farmland. Several roads in the county were flooded. Some of the roads were closed, and some were washed out in spots. The town of Alpena, though not itself flooded, was almost isolated by flooded fields and roads in the area. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the Big Sioux River, and of many small streams and lakes. Widespread flooding of farmland was also reported, including farmland both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. Numerous roads were flooded and some of the roads suffered heavy damage. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate to major flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county. Most notable was flooding of Six Mile Creek in the Brookings area, and flooding around the south end of Lake Poinsett in the northwest part of the county. Farmland was flooded, both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. The Big Sioux River crested at 4.41 feet above flood stage near Brookings on March 24th. Several roads were closed by the flooding, and water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the Big Sioux River. U. S. Highways 81 and 14 were two of the major roads flooded. Some roads were heavily damaged, but the amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. Some homes and businesses were also flooded. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused moderate flooding of the Big Sioux River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county. Farmland was flooded, both near to and away from the Big Sioux River. Other lowlands were flooded, including the Flandreau City Park. The Big Sioux River crested at 2.90 feet above flood stage at Flandreau on March 24th. Several roads were closed by the flooding, and water was running over other roads, from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the Big Sioux River. The flooded roads included numerous rural roads. Some roads were heavily damaged, but the amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. Some homes and businesses were also flooded. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

Snowmelt flooding in March continued across much of central and northeast South Dakota as the rest of the snowpack melted into early April. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland remained flooded. Roads, culverts, and bridges were damaged across the region. Several roads were washed out with many closed. Many homes were threatened with some surrounded by water. Rising lake levels in northeast South Dakota also threatened and flooded many homes. Many people had to use four-wheelers to get to their homes. A Presidential Disaster was declared for all of the counties do to the flooding damage.  The total damage estimates, including March, were from 4.5 to 5 million dollars for the area.  The flooding diminished across much of the area into May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Snake Creek near Redfield continued above flood stage throughout April and into May. The creek rose above the flood stage of 11.5 feet to 17.54 feet in late March.

This had been the worst flooding in their history.  Hecla was also bad with many roads closed.  Many homes were also threatened by flooding in the Hecla and Claremont areas.  The Maple River in northwest Brown county also flooded for a few days in early April. The river went about 2.4 feet above the flood stage of 10 feet.

Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the James River, and of many small streams and lakes. Widespread flooding of farmland was also reported, including farmland both near to and away from the James River. Numerous roads were flooded and some of the roads suffered heavy damage. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the rapid onset of the flooding. The flooding began with warm weather which arrived at the middle of the month, and continued through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting of a heavy winter snow cover caused major flooding of the James River, as well as flooding of small streams and lakes in the county. Farmland was also flooded, both near to and away from the James River. The James River crested at 10.22 feet above flood stage near Yankton on March 28th. Several roads were closed by the flooding including a few major state and county roads. Water was running over other roads from flooded streams, creeks, and fields as well as from the James River. Some roads were heavily damaged, but the amount of damage could not be determined before repairs began. The greatest flooding away from the James River took place in the northern part of the county. Some homes and businesses were also flooded. The flooding onset was rapid for a snow melt flood due to high water and groundwater levels from record precipitation in the year 2010.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread and deep snow pack began to melt in early March across central and northeast South Dakota. The runoff brought many rivers and creeks across the area over flood stage. A Presidential Disaster was declared for much of the area. Much of the flooding continued into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Big Sioux River went above and back below flood stage at the end of March. The river rose above flood stage of 11 feet to 12.87 feet at the gaging site at Castlewood. Minor flooding of agricultural land along with a few roads occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very intense low pressure area moving across North Dakota brought widespread blizzard conditions to central and northeast South Dakota. The low pressure area brought 1 to 3 inches of snowfall to the region. This new snowfall combined with 30 to 50 mph winds with gusts to 60 to 70 mph brought widespread whiteout conditions. Traffic was brought to a standstill with many motorists having to be rescued and taken to shelter. Hundreds of cars were stranded on mainly Highway 12 and Interstate-29. Two people traveling on Highway 10 in McPherson county told about how they became stuck and were picked up by another vehicle and that it took them over 2 1/2 hours to travel just a few miles to safety. Interstate-29 was closed from Watetown to Sisseton from 6 pm on the 11th until noon on the 12th.  Many events were affected including the Girls State Basketball Tournament in Watertown. There were several overturned semi trucks along with several vehicle accidents across the area. Some of the highest wind gusts include, 56 mph at Watertown; 58 mph at Mobridge, Sisseton, and Faulkton; 59 mph at Aberdeen; 61 mph at Bowdle; 66 mph near Hillhead, and 71 mph west of Long Lake.
