Several consecutive days were experienced with an extremely stressful combination of high heat and humidity. Heat indices frequently rose above 115 degrees during the day, with temperatures reaching the 90s and dew points remaining in the 70s to lower 80s. The high heat and humidity were evident at night, with minimum temperatures usually in the middle to upper 70s, and in some cases 80 degrees or a little higher. There were some cases of heat related illnesses in people, and several reports of livestock deaths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of excessive heat produced daytime temperatures reaching the 90s and dew points in the 70s to lower 80s, with heat indices often reaching or exceeding 115 degrees. Nighttime temperatures often in the mid 70s to lower 80s with continued high humidity provided little if any relief. The heat and humidity caused prolonged stress on people and livestock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding from heavy rain in parts of southeast South Dakota north of Interstate 90 before daybreak on July 10th, beginning just before midnight on July 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 7 inches caused flash flooding of numerous fields and several roads.

Major to record Missouri River flooding continued in July with the flooding continuing to have major impacts. Populated areas evacuated before the month remained evacuated, with a few additional evacuations made. Damage continued to many homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas and other lowlands. Highest river levels were close to those in June, with a few record high stages set in July. A very slow general drop in the river began before the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain, affecting mainly the east shore of the Missouri River, also continued to affect parts of the Gregory County shore. A few roads and recreation areas near the river remained flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across part of southeast South Dakota near and north of Interstate 90 during the afternoon of July 1st. Most of the damage from the severe weather and flash flooding was in Lake, Brookings, and Moody Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage, including several trees uprooted or flattened, and others snapped a few feet above the ground. The winds also destroyed a 60 foot by 90 foot shed. Several homes and vehicles were damaged, including a car crushed by a tree and a garage heavily damaged by a falling tree. The winds caused damage to power lines and poles, both directly and from wind blown debris, with a resulting power outage in most of Wentworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major to record Missouri River flooding continued in July with the flooding continuing to have major impacts. Populated areas evacuated before the month remained evacuated, with a few additional evacuations made. Damage continued to many homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas and other lowlands. Highest river levels were close to those in June, with a few record high stages set in July. A very slow general drop in the river began before the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at major levels through July. Lowlands along the river including numerous roads, parks, recreational areas including a golf course, and several homes remained flooded. Thousands of acres of usually dry land, including several farms, remained flooded. The river began a very slow general fall late in the month. The highest stage near Springfield was 4.6 feet above flood stage on July 12th. This was only 0.2 feet below the record crest recorded in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding on the James River continued through the month, with the river varying according to the uneven areas of heavy rainfall. Peak stages were generally a little above the peak stages of June.  Farmland and several roads near the river remained flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the James River, ongoing since the snowmelt season in March, continued through July. Farmland and other lowlands near the river remained flooded, with the water level varying slightly up and down due to sporadic heavy rainfall. The highest stage at Huron was 6.3 feet above flood stage on July 27th, a little higher than the peak stage of June, but not as high as peak levels earlier in the Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major to record Missouri River flooding continued in July with the flooding continuing to have major impacts. Populated areas evacuated before the month remained evacuated, with a few additional evacuations made. Damage continued to many homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas and other lowlands. Highest river levels were close to those in June, with a few record high stages set in July. A very slow general drop in the river began before the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at major levels through July. Lowlands along the river including numerous roads, parks, recreational areas and homes remained flooded. Most of the Dakota Dunes community remained flooded, with many residents and business owners still displaced. Thousands of acres of usually dry land, including several farms, remained flooded. The river began a very slow general fall late in the month. The highest stage near Ponca, Nebraska, was 8.3 feet above flood stage on July 11th. This was equal to the highest level recorded in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With much below normal December precipitation, the severe drought conditions continued across part of northeast South Dakota including the counties of Deuel, Codington, and Hamlin. The severe drought conditions would continue into January.

Heavy upslope snow developed across the northern Black Hills behind a strong cold front. Snowfall amounts of six to 12 inches were reported across the higher elevations, including the Lead and Deadwood areas.

A strong cold front brought gusty winds to portions of western South Dakota. The strongest winds developed across the eastern foothills of the Black Hills and the adjacent plains, where sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph and gusts around 60 mph were recorded.

A strong cold front brought gusty winds to portions of western South Dakota. The strongest winds developed across the eastern foothills of the Black Hills and the adjacent plains, where sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph and gusts around 60 mph were recorded.

Several consecutive days were experienced with an extremely stressful combination of high heat and humidity. Heat indices frequently rose above 115 degrees during the day, with temperatures reaching the 90s and dew points remaining in the 70s to lower 80s. The high heat and humidity were evident at night, with minimum temperatures usually in the middle to upper 70s, and in some cases 80 degrees or a little higher. There were some cases of heat related illnesses in people, and several reports of livestock deaths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of excessive heat produced daytime temperatures reaching the 90s and dew points in the 70s to lower 80s, with heat indices often reaching or exceeding 115 degrees. Nighttime temperatures often in the mid 70s to lower 80s with continued high humidity provided little if any relief. The heat and humidity caused prolonged stress on people and livestock.

Flooding redeveloped on the Big Sioux River due to heavy rains, and subsided before the end of the month, except near Sioux City, where backing of waters from the flooded Missouri River kept the flooding going. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued to worsen for most of June. Lowlands along the river including numerous roads, parks, recreational areas and several homes were flooded. Several roads, homes, and parks in and near Springfield were flooded, including a golf course. Thousands of acres of usually dry land were flooded, with several farms affected and farm equipment damaged. The highest stage near Springfield was a record 4.8 feet above flood stage on June 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days were experienced with an extremely stressful combination of high heat and humidity. Heat indices frequently rose above 115 degrees during the day, with temperatures reaching the 90s and dew points remaining in the 70s to lower 80s. The high heat and humidity were evident at night, with minimum temperatures usually in the middle to upper 70s, and in some cases 80 degrees or a little higher. There were some cases of heat related illnesses in people, and several reports of livestock deaths.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of excessive heat produced daytime temperatures reaching the 90s and dew points in the 70s to lower 80s, with heat indices often reaching or exceeding 115 degrees. Nighttime temperatures often in the mid 70s to lower 80s with continued high humidity provided little if any relief. The heat and humidity caused prolonged stress on people and livestock.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall of 5 to 7 inches brought flash flooding to mainly eastern Lyman county. Many roads were flooded with some washed out. Two women died in two separate vehicles after driving into a washed out portion of a road. One woman was found 100 yards downstream while the other woman was found 4 miles downstream. The accidents were believed to happen between 930 and 10 am. The location was 9 miles north of Reliance on BIA 10 just north of the intersection with Highway 47.

A compact and strong low pressure area along with a strong cold front brought high winds to much of central and parts of northeast South Dakota. The high winds caused some spotty damage across the region to trees and structures. The highest wind gust of 75 mph was recorded in Corson county.

A strong low pressure system to the northwest and a strong high pressure system to the southeast brought very strong south winds across central and north central South Dakota from the late morning until the early evening.  South winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts over 60 mph caused spotty damage across the region. The high winds created large waves on Lake Oahe near Pierre which damaged several docks along with some boats at a marina.  There were also some tree branches downed across the region along with some damage to a few structures. With the high winds, warm temperatures, and low humidity several grassland fires also broke out across parts of the region. The highest wind gust of 68 mph occurred in Corson county.

There were also some tree branches downed across the region along with some damage to a few structures. With the high winds, warm temperatures, and low humidity several grassland fires also broke out across parts of the region. The highest wind gust of 68 mph occurred in Corson county.

Minor flooding of the James River decreased slowly during September, with continued abatement of road, farmland, and other lowland flooding along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River, ongoing since the snowmelt season in March, abated very slowly through September. Flooding of farmland and other lowlands steadily decreased, and very few roads continued to be affected. The highest stage at Huron was 1.8 feet above flood stage on September 1st.

Several wildfires broke out in Gregory and Charles Mix Counties during the four day period. Warm and dry weather, strong winds, and dry vegetation due to extended dry weather preceding this time contributed to the fires starting and spreading. The fires affected grassland and cropland, including baled hay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wildfires damaged grassland and crops. The largest fire started just south of Lake Andes on October 6th and burned about 400 large round hay bales in addition to grassland. One firefighter suffered smoke inhalation fighting this fire. Firefighters were called to the same site the next day as flames sparked up again. No other injuries were reported to humans or livestock. The amount of crop damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several wildfires broke out in Gregory and Charles Mix Counties during the four day period. Warm and dry weather, strong winds, and dry vegetation due to extended dry weather preceding this time contributed to the fires starting and spreading. The fires affected grassland and cropland, including baled hay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wildfires damaged grassland and crops. No injuries to humans or livestock were reported. The largest fire started in a bean field 3 miles south and 1 mile west of Burke, and burned over a one mile stretch before it was brought under control by four fire departments. The amount of crop damage was not known.

A large upper level low pressure area along with deep moisture brought very heavy rains to central, north central, and northeast South Dakota. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches occurred across much of the area resulting in widespread flooding. Many creeks were flooded along with many roads and thousands of acres of crop and pastureland by the two day heavy rain event. Many roads were closed across the area. The two day rainfall amounts were generally more than the normal June rainfall across the region.||Some rainfall amounts across the region included; 3.07 inches at Kennebec; 3.22 inches at Roscoe; 3.45 inches at Ipswich; 3.50 inches near Hosmer; 3.61 inches at Fort Pierre; 3.69 inches at Leola; 3.77 inches near Onida and at Mission Ridge; 3.82 inches near Akaska; 3.92 inches at Westport; 3.96 inches at Seneca; 4.03 inches at Murdo; 4.60 inches at Highmore; 4.63 inches at Miller; 4.70 inches at Pierre and north of Gettysburg; and 4.98 inches east of Hayes.  Locations with more than five inches of rainfall included; 5.07 inches north of Vivian; 5.39 inches southeast of Pierre; 5.72 inches 10 south of Reliance; 7.00 inches near Fort Thompson; and 7.50 inches at Lake Sharpe.

High lake levels from April and May along with above normal June rainfall kept all lakes in Day county at record levels throughout the month. Extensive flooding of land, roads, and many residences continued throughout June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water levels coming into June along with above normal June rainfall kept water levels up on Blue Dog, Bitter, Rush, and Waubay Lakes throughout the month. The high lake levels continued to cause extensive road and property damage.  Many families remained away from their homes and cabins. Also, hundreds of acres of farmland remained flooded along with many roads. Road and property damage would be in the several millions of dollars. The high lake levels and flooding would continue for the next several months.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High lake levels coming into June along with near to above normal June rainfall kept the water levels above normal throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water levels from April and May continued throughout June on Lake Poinsett along with several other lakes throughout Hamlin county. Lake Poinsett, including several other lakes, continued to flood and damage several homes along with several county and township roads.  The flooding severely hampered travel in Hamlin county. Road and property damage would be in the several millions of dollars. High lake levels and flooding would continue for the next several months.

With record snow melt runoff in the upper Missouri River Basin along with much above normal May and June precipitation, dam releases on the Missouri River continued to increase through the middle of June. The Oahe Dam releases were slowly increased to 160,000 CFS. The increased releases from Garrison Dam in North Dakota and Oahe Dam north of Pierre resulted in continued rises on the Missouri River throughout June. The Missouri River at Pierre rose to 19.9 feet on June 22nd almost 7 feet above flood stage. The June 2011 runoff in the Missouri River basin above Sioux City, Iowa was 13.8 million acre feet which was the highest monthly runoff amount since 1898.||The state EOC (Emergency Operations Center) continued to handle all coordination of the flood operations. Sandbagging and levee building continued into early June along the river in Pierre and Fort Pierre.  Several thousand volunteers and National Guard soldiers helped out. The National Guard soldiers also conducted 24-hour levee security and surveillance. The State Highway Patrol helped with traffic control, security, and evacuation notices. A couple Black Hawk helicopters conducted several air operations. Divers were also called upon for repair of levees.||Hundreds of people were evacuated from homes and businesses in low-lying areas. Red Cross shelters were also set up. The storm sewers were plugged in Pierre and Fort Pierre with many large pumps utilized to pump out any new rainfall. Several public areas remained closed and flooded in Pierre and Fort Pierre including several parks and a golf course. Countless numbers of flooded trees would not survive in the parks. Many homes, roads, along with some businesses were flooded in Pierre and Fort Pierre by the rising river. Also, many homes in Pierre and Fort Pierre received groundwater into their basements. Thousands of acres of farm and pastureland were flooded all along the length of the river.||Road damage was extensive from all of the heavy equipment and sandbagging efforts. The road damage along with levee removal would be in the millions of dollars. Lake Oahe north of Pierre to the North Dakota border also rose to record levels affecting several locations. The flood mitigation efforts along with the flood damage would be in the tens of millions of dollars. The flooding continued into July.

The National Guard soldiers also conducted 24-hour levee security and surveillance. The State Highway Patrol helped with traffic control, security, and evacuation notices. A couple Black Hawk helicopters conducted several air operations. Divers were also called upon for repair of levees.||Hundreds of people were evacuated from homes and businesses in low-lying areas. Red Cross shelters were also set up. The storm sewers were plugged in Pierre and Fort Pierre with many large pumps utilized to pump out any new rainfall. Several public areas remained closed and flooded in Pierre and Fort Pierre including several parks and a golf course. Countless numbers of flooded trees would not survive in the parks. Many homes, roads, along with some businesses were flooded in Pierre and Fort Pierre by the rising river. Also, many homes in Pierre and Fort Pierre received groundwater into their basements. Thousands of acres of farm and pastureland were flooded all along the length of the river.||Road damage was extensive from all of the heavy equipment and sandbagging efforts. The road damage along with levee removal would be in the millions of dollars. Lake Oahe north of Pierre to the North Dakota border also rose to record levels affecting several locations. The flood mitigation efforts along with the flood damage would be in the tens of millions of dollars. The flooding continued into July.

Thunderstorms produced damaging winds and flash flooding from heavy rain in parts of southeast South Dakota north of Interstate 90 before daybreak on July 10th, beginning just before midnight on July 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall measured at 6 inches caused flash flooding of numerous roads in and near Dell Rapids, and caused flooding of Rocky Run Creek, inundating South Dakota Highway 115 on the south end of Dell Rapids and forcing its closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain over several days caused flooding along the Bad River from Cottonwood to Philip. Several roads near the river were covered by water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork of the Bad River and Cottonwood Creek flooded due to runoff from heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred over portions of western Custer County when a slow moving thunderstorm produced two inches of rain in less than an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flash flooding along sections of Pleasant Valley Road, Star Ridge Road, Nestle Rock Road, Steverson Loop, and along Dewey Road at Red Creek and Coon Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm brought some large hail to Clark county in northeast South Dakota.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved across northwestern South Dakota, producing wind gusts to 70 mph from Harding and Butte Counties, southeastward into central South Dakota. A stock dam failed due to heavy rain and caused flash flooding in northeastern Jackson and southeastern Haakon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain flooded a 600 foot section of SD Highway 63 north of Interstate 90, washed out several rural roads, and caused a stock dam to fail. Significant flooding occurred along Ash and Brave Bull Creeks in northeastern Jackson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced wind gusts to 60 mph near Porcupine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 60 mph were reported east of Porcupine.

Moderate to major flooding on the James River continued in early August, with the flooding very slowly abating during the month. Peak stages were in the first few days of the month. Flooding of farmland and several roads near the river very slowly eased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the James River, ongoing since the snowmelt season in March, continued into early August, with the flooding continuing but very slowly abating through the month. Flooding of farmland and other lowlands near the river very slowly abated. The highest stage near Mitchell was 4.6 feet above flood stage on August 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds in part of southeast South Dakota, including Beadle, Davison, and Hutchinson Counties, in the late afternoon and early evening of August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down trees and power lines in Freeman, causing a power outage. The winds also caused minor roof damage to several structures. More substantial damage was caused to a few homes, mainly roofs, by falling trees and large limbs. Fallen trees and tree debris blocked some roads in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail at numerous locations in southeast South Dakota, south of Interstate 90, on August 18th, mostly during the late morning and afternoon. There were also a few reports of damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage, including numerous trees blown down. Some homes were damaged by falling trees or tree debris. The winds drove hail that was up to golf ball size, and with the hail contributed to crop damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail at numerous locations in southeast South Dakota, south of Interstate 90, on August 18th, mostly during the late morning and afternoon. There were also a few reports of damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail up to golf ball size, driven by strong to damaging winds, caused widespread property and crop damage in and near Yankton. Numerous windows were broken, both in structures and in vehicles, roofs and siding were damaged in several homes and businesses, and trees were stripped. Corn and other crops in the area were heavily damaged, with crops totally stripped in some areas. The amount of crop damage could not yet be determined.

The storms produced large hail and gusty winds across much of the area. Two to three inches of rain fell over northern and central Harding County and produced flash flooding.

Historic releases on the Oahe Dam of 160,000 CFS kept the Missouri River from Pierre to Chamberlain at record flood levels throughout July. Extensive sandbagging and levee building had been done earlier to hold back the river. Residents in the Pierre, Fort Pierre, and Oacoma areas continued to be the most affected by the river. Many homes along with roads, crop and pastureland remained flooded throughout the month. The Missouri River at Pierre remained from 5 to 6 foot above flood stage throughout July. The Missouri River at Chamberlain reached a record stage of 75.1 feet on July 8th. Flood stage at Chamberlain is 65 feet. The flooding on the river began in late May and continued into August. For additional information, see May and June Storm Data entries.

Monthly rainfall amounts from 4 to almost 7 inches kept many areas flooded across Brown, Spink, and Edmunds counties throughout July. Several roads along with crop and pastureland remained flooded. Both Moccasin Creek and Foote Creek in Brown county went above flood stage in July. A portion of U.S. Highway 12 in Edmunds county remained flooded throughout July.  In northeast Brown county, the Hecla and Claremont areas continued to have extensive flooding issues. In Spink county, the Snake Creek near Redfield fell below flood stage on July 7th. Some July rainfall amounts included; 4.05 inches at Doland; 4.71 inches at Roscoe; 5.40 inches at Groton; 6.19 inches at Ipswich; and 6.63 inches at Aberdeen. The flooding diminished throughout August across all of the region as below normal rainfall occurred.
Hail to golf ball size damaged automobiles and caused other minor damage in Custer.
Hail to golf ball size damaged automobiles and caused other minor damage in Custer.

A large upper level high pressure area built over the region bringing very hot and humid conditions.  This was the worst heat wave to hit the region since July 2006. Beginning on Friday July 15th and persisting through Wednesday July 20th, many locations experienced high temperatures in the 90s to lower 100s, with low temperatures in the 70s at night. In addition, humidity levels rose to extreme levels at times.  Surface dew point temperatures in the 70s and lower 80s brought extreme heat index values of up to 110 to 125 degrees.  The dewpoints were some of the highest ever recorded in the region. The dewpoint at Aberdeen tied the previous record with 82 degrees. Sisseton also tied their record with 83 degrees. Watertown came a degree shy of tying their record with 80 degrees. ||The prolonged heat took its toll on livestock with fifteen hundred cattle perishing during the heat.  Numerous sports and outdoor activities were cancelled. Some of the highest heat index values included; 110 degrees at Mobridge; 111 degrees at Watertown; 113 degrees at Miller and Gettysburg; 114 degrees at Wheaton and Faulkton; 116 degrees at Pierre; 118 degrees at Sisseton; and 121 degrees at Aberdeen.  The highest heat index value occurred at Leola with a temperature of 98 degrees and a dewpoint of 82 degrees, the heat index hit 125 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major impacts from Missouri River flooding continued into early August, with flooding varying from minor to major, and evacuated areas remaining evacuated. The water levels receded very slowly during the month, and effects of the flooding began to abate, also very slowly. However in many places, extent of the damage to homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas, and other lowlands was beginning to become evident. Highest river levels were very early in August; in most cases on the first day of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at  moderate levels at the start of August.  Lowlands along the river including numerous roads, parks, recreational areas and several homes remained flooded. Thousands of acres of usually dry land, including several farms, remained flooded. The river fell slowly during the month. The highest stage at Gayville was 1.4 feet above flood stage on August 1st.

A large upper level high pressure area built over the region bringing very hot and humid conditions.  This was the worst heat wave to hit the region since July 2006. Beginning on Friday July 15th and persisting through Wednesday July 20th, many locations experienced high temperatures in the 90s to lower 100s, with low temperatures in the 70s at night. In addition, humidity levels rose to extreme levels at times.  Surface dew point temperatures in the 70s and lower 80s brought extreme heat index values of up to 110 to 125 degrees.  The dewpoints were some of the highest ever recorded in the region. The dewpoint at Aberdeen tied the previous record with 82 degrees. Sisseton also tied their record with 83 degrees. Watertown came a degree shy of tying their record with 80 degrees. ||The prolonged heat took its toll on livestock with fifteen hundred cattle perishing during the heat.  Numerous sports and outdoor activities were cancelled. Some of the highest heat index values included; 110 degrees at Mobridge; 111 degrees at Watertown; 113 degrees at Miller and Gettysburg; 114 degrees at Wheaton and Faulkton; 116 degrees at Pierre; 118 degrees at Sisseton; and 121 degrees at Aberdeen.  The highest heat index value occurred at Leola with a temperature of 98 degrees and a dewpoint of 82 degrees, the heat index hit 125 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major impacts from Missouri River flooding continued into early August, with flooding varying from minor to major, and evacuated areas remaining evacuated. The water levels receded very slowly during the month, and effects of the flooding began to abate, also very slowly. However in many places, extent of the damage to homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas, and other lowlands was beginning to become evident. Highest river levels were very early in August; in most cases on the first day of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at moderate to major levels at the start of August. Lowlands along the river including numerous roads, parks, recreational areas including a golf course, and several homes remaining flooded. Thousands of acres of usually dry land, including several farms, remained flooded. The river fell slowly during most of the month. The highest stage near Springfield was 3.9 feet above flood stage on August 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major impacts from Missouri River flooding continued into early August, with flooding varying from minor to major, and evacuated areas remaining evacuated. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at moderate to major levels at the start of August.  Lowlands along the river east of Lewis and Clark Lake including numerous roads, parks, recreational areas and several homes remained flooded. The flooding included the Larson's Landing development west of Yankton with most homes and recreational vehicles there remaining flooded. Thousands of acres of usually dry land, including several farms, remained flooded.  The river fell slowly during the month. The highest stage at Yankton was 5.5 feet above flood stage on August 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding redeveloped on the Big Sioux River due to heavy rains, and subsided before the end of the month, except near Sioux City, where backing of waters from the flooded Missouri River kept the flooding going. The flooding was minor, except along part of the lower Big Sioux River south of Sioux Falls, where brief moderate to major flooding was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding redeveloped along the Big Sioux River, then subsided during the month. Lowlands along the river were flooded, including farmland and several roads. The highest stages were 5.0 feet above flood stage at the Hawarden, Iowa gage, and 4.0 feet above flood stage at Akron, Iowa, both on July 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major to record Missouri River flooding continued in July with the flooding continuing to have major impacts. Populated areas evacuated before the month remained evacuated, with a few additional evacuations made. Damage continued to many homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas and other lowlands. Highest river levels were close to those in June, with a few record high stages set in July. A very slow general drop in the river began before the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at major levels through July. Lowlands along the river east of Lewis and Clark Lake including numerous roads, parks, recreational areas and several homes remained flooded. The flooding included the Larson's Landing development west of Yankton with most homes and recreational vehicles there remaining flooded. Thousands of acres of usually dry land, including several farms, remained flooded. The river began a very slow general fall late in the month. The highest stage at Yankton was 6.1 feet above flood stage on July 7th. This was equal to the highest level recorded in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding on the James River continued through the month, with the river varying according to the uneven areas of heavy rainfall. Peak stages were generally a little above the peak stages of June.  Farmland and several roads near the river remained flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the James River, ongoing since the snowmelt season in March, continued through July. Farmland and other lowlands near the river remained flooded, with the water level varying slightly up and down due to sporadic heavy rainfall. The highest stage near Forestburg was 4.0 feet above flood stage on July 1st, slightly higher than the peak stage of June, but not as high as peak levels earlier in the Spring.

Moderate to major flooding on the James River continued through the month, with the river varying according to the uneven areas of heavy rainfall. Peak stages were generally a little above the peak stages of June.  Farmland and several roads near the river remained flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to major flooding of the James River, ongoing since the snowmelt season in March, continued through July. Farmland and other lowlands near the river remained flooded, with the water level varying slightly up and down due to sporadic heavy rainfall. The highest stage near Mitchell was 4.9 feet above flood stage on July 3rd, slightly higher than the peak stage of June, but not as high as peak levels earlier in the Spring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major to record Missouri River flooding continued in July with the flooding continuing to have major impacts. Populated areas evacuated before the month remained evacuated, with a few additional evacuations made. Damage continued to many homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas and other lowlands. Highest river levels were close to those in June, with a few record high stages set in July. A very slow general drop in the river began before the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at major to record levels through July. Lowlands along the river including numerous roads and recreational areas and some homes remained flooded. The river began a very slow general fall late in the month. The highest stage near Greenwood was a record 8.9 feet above flood stage on July 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level high pressure area built over the region bringing very hot and humid conditions.  This was the worst heat wave to hit the region since July 2006. Beginning on Friday July 15th and persisting through Wednesday July 20th, many locations experienced high temperatures in the 90s to lower 100s, with low temperatures in the 70s at night. In addition, humidity levels rose to extreme levels at times.  Surface dew point temperatures in the 70s and lower 80s brought extreme heat index values of up to 110 to 125 degrees.  The dewpoints were some of the highest ever recorded in the region. The dewpoint at Aberdeen tied the previous record with 82 degrees. Sisseton also tied their record with 83 degrees. Watertown came a degree shy of tying their record with 80 degrees. ||The prolonged heat took its toll on livestock with fifteen hundred cattle perishing during the heat.  Numerous sports and outdoor activities were cancelled. Some of the highest heat index values included; 110 degrees at Mobridge; 111 degrees at Watertown; 113 degrees at Miller and Gettysburg; 114 degrees at Wheaton and Faulkton; 116 degrees at Pierre; 118 degrees at Sisseton; and 121 degrees at Aberdeen.  The highest heat index value occurred at Leola with a temperature of 98 degrees and a dewpoint of 82 degrees, the heat index hit 125 degrees.
Severe winds affected north central South Dakota during the late evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated.

Historic releases on the Oahe Dam of 160,000 CFS kept the Missouri River from Pierre to Chamberlain at record flood levels throughout July. Extensive sandbagging and levee building had been done earlier to hold back the river. Residents in the Pierre, Fort Pierre, and Oacoma areas continued to be the most affected by the river. Many homes along with roads, crop and pastureland remained flooded throughout the month. The Missouri River at Pierre remained from 5 to 6 foot above flood stage throughout July. The Missouri River at Chamberlain reached a record stage of 75.1 feet on July 8th. Flood stage at Chamberlain is 65 feet. The flooding on the river began in late May and continued into August. For additional information, see May and June Storm Data entries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms across parts of Northern Roberts county produced anywhere from 4 to 8 inches of rainfall resulting in flash flooding.  The town of New Effington was affected once again with many roads along with several homes flooded. Sandbagging took place to keep the water from the school. Highway 127 from New Effington to Hammer was flooded in several spots. This was the second time that New Effington was flooded in the last month. The floodwaters remained for several days afterward with several roads flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage in Flandreau, including numerous trees uprooted or snapped. Several homes and vehicles were damaged, mostly by falling trees or tree debris. A large camper in the city park was overturned. The winds and tree debris also damaged power lines, causing a power outage. Tree debris blocked numerous roads in and near town. Damage to the city's electrical system alone was later estimated at $125,000.

Missouri River flooding decreased steadily during September, and ended over most of the river late in the month. The flooding varied from minor to major at the start of the month, as significant flooding continued to the start of September in some areas, but no new flooding was reported. The decreasing and ending of the flooding continued to reveal effects and damage caused by the record floods earlier in the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain decreased steadily, and ended late in the month. Flooding of roads and recreational areas ended. The highest stage near Greenwood was 3.5 feet above flood stage on September 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across part of southeast South Dakota near and north of Interstate 90 during the afternoon of July 1st. Most of the damage from the severe weather and flash flooding was in Lake, Brookings, and Moody Counties.

Flooding of lakes and lowlands in several counties in southeast South Dakota continued through April. The flooding included farmland and other lowlands, with some roads flooded and damaged. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the slowness of any improvement in the flooding situation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of lakes and lowlands, including some farmland, continued in the county through April. While flooding of small streams abated, lake and lowland flooding continued with very slow improvement. Several roads remained flooded. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that the flooding either grew worse or improved so slowly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced hail to quarter size across northern Jackson County.

Record snow melt along with much above normal May and June precipitation in the upper Missouri River basin resulted in record high releases on the Oahe Dam upstream. Due to the high releases, the Missouri River at Oacoma and Chamberlain rose to above the flood stage of 65 feet on June 12th. The river rose to a record 74.6 feet on June 30th. Many people along the river, especially in Oacoma, had to build levees to hold back the rising water with some locations being flooded.  The flooding continued into July.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved from northeastern Wyoming across the northern Black Hills and west central South Dakota plains. Hail larger than the size of baseballs and wind gusts to 70 mph caused significant damage to buildings and automobiles and power outages across much of Rapid City.

Thunderstorms produced flash flooding and damaging winds at several locations in southeast South Dakota during the evening of June 20th. The thunderstorms affected the area from the Missouri River to Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused flash flooding of several fields and roads, including sections of South Dakota Highways 44 and 50.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado did not cause any damage. Size and location were based on pictures and reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of the James River decreased slowly during September, with continued abatement of road, farmland, and other lowland flooding along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the James River, ongoing since the snowmelt season in March, abated very slowly through September. Flooding of farmland and other lowlands steadily decreased, and very few roads continued to be affected. The highest stage near Mitchell was  2.3 feet above flood stage on September 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused brief moderate flooding of Split Rock Creek in mid July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused moderate flooding of Split Rock Creek, with lowlands along the creek and a few county roads affected. The creek crested at 3.0 feet above flood stage at Corson on July 15th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded by nearly 4 inches of rain.

A severe thunderstorm developed in the Rapid City area and moved eastward across the plains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred along the Cheyenne River. The river gage at the SD Highway 34/73 bridge southeast of Plainview crested at 18.5 feet, or 2.5 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure area moving east along with a strong warm front surging north brought numerous severe thunderstorms to north central and northeast South Dakota. Damaging winds, large hail, flash flooding, along with a few tornadoes all occurred with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighty mph winds downed several trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over southwestern Harding and northern Butte Counties, then tracked eastward across northern Meade, southern Perkins, and central Ziebach Counties. Hail to the size of baseballs and 80 mph wind gusts caused minor damage across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms brought large hail and strong winds to parts of central and northeast South Dakota. Also, a funnel cloud was reported.
A line of severe thunderstorms produced large hail and strong wind gusts across central and eastern Perkins County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten inch diameter trees were uprooted by the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought large hail, damaging winds, along with a few tornadoes to parts of north central and northeast South Dakota.

A line of severe thunderstorms developed across Haakon County and moved southeast into south central South Dakota. The storms produced large hail and strong wind gusts across portions of Haakon, Mellette, and Tripp Counties.
An isolated thunderstorm brought sixty mph winds to part of Corson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty mph winds were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding of lakes and lowlands in several counties in southeast South Dakota continued through April. The flooding included farmland and other lowlands, with some roads flooded and damaged. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year contributed to the slowness of any improvement in the flooding situation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of lakes and lowlands, including Lake Madison and farmland, continued in the county through April. While flooding of small streams abated, lake and lowland flooding continued with very slow improvement. Many roads remained flooded, with additional damage. High water and groundwater levels resulting from record precipitation in the previous year was the main reason that the flooding either grew worse or improved so slowly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm became severe for a brief time over the northern Black Hills and produced hail to golf ball size.

A long-lived supercell thunderstorm developed over southwestern Harding and northern Butte Counties, then tracked eastward across northern Meade, southern Perkins, and central Ziebach Counties. Hail to the size of baseballs and 80 mph wind gusts caused minor damage across the area.
A severe thunderstorm produced strong wind gusts in the Oglala area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed across the southern Black Hills and moved northeastward to Rapid City and across Meade County. The storms produced large hail and wind gusts around 60 mph.

Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds over an area that extended from Huron and White lake southeast to the far southeast corner of the state in Union County. The severe weather took place during the morning hours, starting before daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding from May along with much above normal June rainfall kept parts of northeast South Dakota flooded throughout June. The counties mainly affected were Brown, Spink, and Edmunds. Crop and pastureland along with many roads remained flooded throughout the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many areas of Brown county continued to experience flooding throughout June from a very wet spring and much above normal rainfall in June. The main areas of concern continued to be in northeast Brown county in the Hecla and Claremont areas. Many roads along with countless acres of crop and pastureland remained under water throughout the county. Very heavy rainfall brought both the Moccasin and Foot Creeks above flood stage for the end of the month. Moccasin Creek rose to about a half foot above flood stage while Foot creek rose to 1.3 feet above flood stage. The flooding continued into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across northwestern South Dakota, producing wind gusts to 70 mph from Harding and Butte Counties, southeastward into central South Dakota. A stock dam failed due to heavy rain and caused flash flooding in northeastern Jackson and southeastern Haakon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred along Ash and Brave Bull Creeks south of Midland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms brought large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding to parts of central and northeast South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of rain had created flash flooding of many roads throughout Pierre.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved north from Nebraska to the Oglala area in southern Shannon County, producing hail to golf ball size and wind gusts around 70 mph.
Strong wind gusts accompanied a thunderstorm near Buffalo.
Quarter sized hail fell over southeastern Meade and far northeastern Pennington Counties.
A line of severe thunderstorms moved across Perkins County.

Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across part of southeast South Dakota near and north of Interstate 90 during the afternoon of July 1st. Most of the damage from the severe weather and flash flooding was in Lake, Brookings, and Moody Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail, in combination with heavy rain, caused damage to soybean crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Major to record Missouri River flooding continued in July with the flooding continuing to have major impacts. Populated areas evacuated before the month remained evacuated, with a few additional evacuations made. Damage continued to many homes, businesses, parks, recreation areas and other lowlands. Highest river levels were close to those in June, with a few record high stages set in July. A very slow general drop in the river began before the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from upstream spring snowmelt and subsequent heavy rain continued at major to record levels through July. Lowlands along the river including several roads and recreational areas remained flooded. The river began a very slow general fall late in the month. The highest stage at Chamberlain was a record 10.1 feet above flood stage on July 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds in part of southeast South Dakota, including Beadle, Davison, and Hutchinson Counties, in the late afternoon and early evening of August 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines and caused tree damage, including several trees blown down.
